summary: McKenzie identifies a business opportunity for a micro-ISV to clone Payloadz's digital delivery service but built on Google Checkout instead of PayPal. The core appeal is Google Checkout's 2% cost advantage through its AdWords tie-in, where every $1 spent on AdWords offsets $10 in processing fees. He also debates whether to build his own delivery system to replace Payloadz, concluding the $15/month savings isn't worth the effort but the ability to customize post-purchase redirects might be. The post closes with a resigned acknowledgment that success in his business may depend more on adapting to Google's ecosystem than on product quality.
key_points: Google Checkout offered a meaningful cost advantage over PayPal through its AdWords credit tie-in, effectively eliminating processing fees for AdWords advertisers
Build-vs-buy decisions for early micro-ISVs should weigh not just cost savings but also the flexibility limitations of third-party tools
McKenzie already recognized Google's outsized influence on small software businesses, noting success would come from adapting to Google's ecosystem as much as from product quality
money_quotes: I would gladly opt for Google Checkout over Paypal if customers could be convinced to use it, for one reason and one reason alone: its going to be 2% extra profit on every sale because of their tie-in with AdWords.
The question is: does saving perhaps $15 a month justify the amount of effort it will take me to hack together some Perl scripts to do exactly the same thing Payloadz does? Answer: No.

Is the hacking justified by it letting me get around stupid limitations in the Payloadz UI, like being unable to redirect my customers to my site after the order has completed? Answer: Quite possibly.
I think if this ever becomes successful it will be as much because I successfully adapted to The Big G as because I have a quality piece of software.
key_insight: For early-stage micro-ISVs, adapting to the dominant platform ecosystem matters as much as product quality, and payment processing economics can meaningfully shift which platforms win.
